title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
text:
What this is
Fifty consecutive days, each sized for three to four focused hours. It follows one rule the whole way through: you do not get to skip backpropagation, tokenization, or attention. Those three are where people who “know LLMs” turn out not to, and they are cheap to learn properly now and expensive to fake later.
The first four weeks you build everything yourself — an autograd engine, a bigram model, a byte-pair encoder, a decoder-only transformer. Only after that do you touch Hugging Face, because the library is much easier to reason about once you already know what it is hiding. Weeks five through seven cover the pretrained ecosystem, scaling laws, post-training, retrieval, and evaluation, carried by one applied project on PubMed abstracts that starts on day 33 and is still running on day 50.

title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
text:
How a day works
Every day page has the same seven parts, in this order:







Part
What it’s for




Why today
One sentence on what this day unlocks. Read it before you start.


What you should be able to do tonight
Two or three checkable claims. Not “understand attention”.


Tasks
Three to five items with minute estimates. Tick them off; the page remembers.


Resources
Every link the day needs. Nothing you have to go hunting for.


Deliverable
The artifact you should have at the end — a file, a notebook, a plot, a paragraph.


Checkpoint
One question. If you can’t answer it without notes, you are not done.


Prepare for tomorrow
Ten minutes tonight. This is what the evening email sends you.

title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
text:
The seven phases


Days 1–7 #### Foundations Linear algebra, calculus, autograd. Karpathy’s micrograd, built twice — the second time from an empty file. Softmax regression as multinomial logistic regression. A two-layer MLP with a backward pass you wrote yourself.


Days 8–14 #### Language models N-grams, perplexity and smoothing before any neural machinery. Then makemore parts 1–3: a bigram count table and a one-layer network computing the same thing, then an MLP, then how to debug a network that trains badly.


Days 15–21 #### Tokens and attention Manual backprop through a whole network. BPE implemented from scratch and trained on your own PubMed corpus. Then the transformer on paper — the textbook chapter and the original paper — with no transformer code yet.


Days 22–28 #### Build a GPT Follow Karpathy through a decoder-only transformer, then rebuild it with the video closed. Sweep the hyperparameters and report a table. Read nanoGPT and diff it against yours. Milestone 1.


Days 29–35 #### Pretrained models The Hugging Face course, chapters 1–3. Encoder versus decoder, and when an encoder is still right. The applied project starts: PubMed abstracts, a prompting baseline first, then a fine-tune. Milestone 2.


Days 36–42 #### Scaling and post-training GPT-3, scaling laws, Chinchilla — read critically, and redo the compute arithmetic yourself. InstructGPT for why a base model isn’t an assistant. LoRA, with your hands on it.


Days 43–50 #### Pipeline, RAG, evaluation DPO. nanochat end to end, which is the only place you see all the stages at once. Retrieval over your abstracts. Then evaluation, which is the part that matters most for clinical work. Capstone on day 50.

title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
section: Five rules that make this work
text:
Five rules that make this work

Reproduce, don’t watch. A Karpathy lecture you watched is worth roughly nothing. Type it out, run it, break it. If a day’s tasks say “rebuild from an empty file”, that instruction is the day.
Change one thing. After you reproduce something, change the model width, the context length, the learning rate, or the sampling temperature, and predict what happens before you run it. Being wrong is the useful part.
Produce the deliverable. Every day names an artifact. If you finish a day with nothing on disk, the day did not happen.
Answer the checkpoint out loud. It takes ninety seconds and it is the only honest signal you have about whether you learned the thing or watched someone else know it.
Catch-up days are load-bearing. Days 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42 and 49 are deliberately not full. They exist because you will fall behind somewhere, and a plan with no slack is a plan you abandon in week three. If you are on track, take the day off or go deeper. Do not pull work forward into them by default.

title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
text:
The evening email
Every evening at about 8:10 pm you get one email: what tomorrow is, and the two-to-four things worth doing tonight so tomorrow starts clean — download the PDF, clone the repo, start the dataset downloading, confirm the GPU is awake. Nothing in that email takes more than ten minutes. It is the same content as the Prepare for tomorrow block at the bottom of each day page.

title: 50-Day LLM Bootcamp
text:
Start here
Tonight: set up your environment — Python, PyTorch, a working GPU or Colab runtime, and two accounts. About an hour, and it is the difference between day 1 being a study day and day 1 being an afternoon of dependency errors.
Then: Day 1, or the full 50 days.

title: Day 49 — Catch-up, and an evaluation harness for your own task
text: ← Day 48 · All 50 days · Day 50 →

title: Day 49 — Catch-up, and an evaluation harness for your own task
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

eval_harness.py runs end to end on a model named at the command line and writes both a metric summary and per-example predictions to disk.
Your held-out split has never been used for prompt iteration, threshold selection or checkpoint selection, and you can say from your logs why you are sure.
Your error taxonomy has counts, was built by reading real outputs rather than summaries, and includes a category for cases where the gold label is wrong.

title: Day 49 — Catch-up, and an evaluation harness for your own task
text:
Tasks 160 min


 30 min Pick the single most incomplete thing from Days 43-48 and finish that one. Not all of them - one. If everything is done, skip straight to the harness.


 60 min Write eval_harness.py taking (model, split) and returning at least three metrics: one accuracy-type, one calibration-type such as ECE or Brier score, and one cost-type such as median latency or tokens per example. Reuse the metric code from Day 35 rather than rewriting it, and dump per-example predictions with IDs so errors are traceable.


 35 min Run the harness across your prompted baseline, your fine-tuned model, and one of your LoRA or RAG variants on the held-out split. Put the three metric rows in one table and resist adding a combined score.


 35 min Read 30 actual wrong outputs - the text, not the summary statistics - and build the error taxonomy by hand. Give every category a count and a verbatim example, and keep the count of cases where the model was right and the label was wrong.

title: Day 47 — Milestone 4 - RAG over your abstract corpus, measured in two halves
text: ← Day 46 · All 50 days · Day 48 →

title: Day 47 — Milestone 4 - RAG over your abstract corpus, measured in two halves
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can report recall@1, recall@5 and MRR for your retriever with no reference to any generated answer.
You can report a gold-context ceiling and a no-context floor on the same questions, and say what fraction of the gap between them your retriever actually closes.
You can split your end-to-end errors into retrieval failures and generation failures with counts, and defend the assignment of any single case.

title: Day 47 — Milestone 4 - RAG over your abstract corpus, measured in two halves
text:
Tasks 210 min


 55 min Chunk and embed the corpus. Decide explicitly between whole-abstract chunks and sentence windows and write down why - for 250-word abstracts this is a real choice, not a default. Embed with your bi-encoder, L2-normalize, and build a FAISS IndexFlatIP so that inner product is cosine and the search is exact; save the ID mapping alongside the index.


 35 min Build a 30-query evaluation set with known relevant abstract IDs. Check for the leak that ruins this kind of eval: if a query was written by paraphrasing one abstract, that abstract is trivially retrievable and your recall is inflated. Mark any such query and keep them in a separate bucket.


 45 min Evaluate the retriever alone. Compute recall@1, recall@5, recall@20 and MRR for the dense index and for yesterday’s BM25 baseline over the same 30 queries. Report both, and note where they disagree - the queries where BM25 wins usually contain an exact gene or drug symbol.


 50 min Wire retrieval into the generator and evaluate end to end using the Milestone 2 metrics from Day 35. Report the retrieval table and the end-to-end table as two separate tables with no combined score anywhere in the document.


 25 min Run the two ablations that make the end-to-end number interpretable: feed the correct abstract directly with no retriever (the ceiling), and feed no context at all (the floor). Record both.

title: Day 45 — Milestone 3 - nanochat, part 2: SFT, RL, eval, serving
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can follow one training example from raw web text through tokenization, base pretraining, mid-training, SFT and RL, saying what changes about it at each hop.
You can explain what the KV cache stores, why decode is cheap relative to prefill once it exists, and what it costs in memory as context grows.

title: Day 45 — Milestone 3 - nanochat, part 2: SFT, RL, eval, serving
text:
Tasks 205 min


 45 min Read chat_sft.py. Work out the conversation format, which special tokens delimit turns, and exactly which token positions contribute to the loss. Then find where mid-training happens relative to SFT and write two sentences on what mid-training is for that SFT is not.


 40 min Read chat_rl.py. Identify the task it optimizes, where the reward comes from, and how the update rule differs from both the DPO loss you derived on Day 43 and the PPO setup in InstructGPT. Note why this stage is applied to a narrow task rather than to chat in general.


 35 min Read chat_eval.py and the leaderboard file in dev/. List the tasks it scores and how each is scored, then put that list next to your Milestone 2 report from Day 35 and mark which of your metrics have no counterpart here and which of theirs have no counterpart in yours.


 40 min Read chat_cli.py and infer_bench.py. Find where the KV cache is allocated and updated, separate the prefill path from the decode path, and write down the tokens/sec numbers infer_bench.py reports along with what would make each of them worse.


 45 min Write nanochat_pipeline.md: the full trace from raw text to served model, one section per stage, each with input, output, time, cost, and one sentence naming the day in Weeks 1-6 where you built the same component yourself.

title: Day 43 — DPO: preference learning as a classification loss
text: ← Day 42 · All 50 days · Day 44 →

title: Day 43 — DPO: preference learning as a classification loss
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can write the DPO loss from memory and say exactly where the partition function Z(x) goes and why it cancels.
You can state what the frozen reference policy is doing in the loss, and predict what generations look like as beta goes to zero.
You can read TRL’s rewards/margins and rewards/accuracies curves and say whether a run learned the preference or just drifted away from the reference.

title: Day 43 — DPO: preference learning as a classification loss
text:
Tasks 200 min


 45 min Read the DPO paper (arXiv 2305.18290) Sections 1-3, stopping at the end of the preliminaries. You already know the RLHF pipeline from InstructGPT in Week 6 - read Section 3 as a restatement of it in notation you will need next.


 45 min Work Section 4 on paper without reading it first. Start from the KL-constrained reward objective, get the closed-form optimal policy, invert it to write the reward as betalog(pi/pi_ref) + betalog Z(x), substitute into Bradley-Terry, and watch Z(x) disappear from the difference. This is the same move as a stratum intercept dropping out of a conditional logistic likelihood for matched pairs - and what is left is a binary logistic regression on the difference of two log-ratios.


 75 min Run a DPOTrainer job in TRL on 200-400 preference pairs, using a model small enough to hold twice in memory since the frozen reference is a second copy. Log rewards/chosen, rewards/rejected, rewards/margins and rewards/accuracies, and plot all four against step.


 35 min Rerun with beta at 0.01 and at 0.5, sample 10 generations from each checkpoint, and write down what actually changed in the text - not just what the loss curve did.

title: Day 41 — LoRA: fine-tuning without the optimizer state
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Predict, before you run anything, the ratio of peak GPU memory between the Day 34 full fine-tune and a LoRA run, and justify the prediction in terms of Adam’s optimizer state rather than the weights themselves.
State what r and alpha each control, and why the update is scaled by alpha/r.
Report trainable parameter count, peak memory, wall-clock and your Day 35 metrics at r = 2, 8 and 32 against the Day 34 baseline.

title: Day 41 — LoRA: fine-tuning without the optimizer state
text:
Tasks 190 min


 55 min Read LoRA Section 1, Section 4 (the W0 + BA parametrization, B initialized to zero and A Gaussian, the alpha/r scaling, and which Transformer matrices they adapt), and Section 7 - 7.1 on which matrices matter, 7.2 on how small r can get away with being, 7.3 on what the update amplifies. Skip the GLUE tables in Section 5.


 15 min Write your predictions down before touching the GPU: peak memory ratio, wall-clock ratio, and the accuracy delta on your held-out set. Date them. You will be wrong about at least one and the point is to know which.


 80 min Wrap the Day 34 training script with PEFT - LoraConfig(r=8, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=[...]), get_peft_model, then print_trainable_parameters() - keeping the same base model, same split and same seed as Day 34. Run r = 2, 8 and 32 with lora_alpha fixed at 16, recording torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() and wall-clock for each.


 40 min Fill in a four-row table - Day 34 full fine-tune, r = 2, r = 8, r = 32 - with trainable parameters, trainable fraction, peak memory, wall-clock, saved checkpoint size on disk, and your Day 35 metrics. Write three sentences naming which prediction was wrong and why.

title: Day 39 — Post-training: how a next-token predictor becomes an assistant
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Explain, without notes, what instruction tuning changes about a model and what it leaves untouched.
Write the Bradley-Terry preference model from 9.2.2 and say why it is the same paired-comparison likelihood you would fit for ranked outcomes anywhere else.
State the difference between PPO (9.3.1) and DPO (9.3.2) in terms of what each one actually trains.

title: Day 39 — Post-training: how a next-token predictor becomes an assistant
text:
Tasks 190 min


 45 min Read 9.1 Instruction Tuning, including 9.1.1 on where instruction data comes from and 9.1.2 on why honest evaluation of an instruction-tuned model requires leaving out whole task clusters rather than held-out examples within a task.


 45 min Read 9.2 Learning from Preferences carefully - 9.2.1 on preference data, 9.2.2 on modelling preferences, 9.2.3 on learning to score them. Derive the pairwise loss -log sigmoid(r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l)) from the Bradley-Terry model yourself before checking it against the chapter.


 40 min Read 9.3 (PPO, DPO, evaluation, and 9.3.4 on what preference-based learning cannot fix) and 9.4 on test-time compute, including 9.4.1 on chain-of-thought prompting.


 60 min Take 20 cases from your Day 35 failure taxonomy - the ones your Day 33 prompt got wrong - and rerun them with a chain-of-thought prompt in the style of 9.4.1. Record how many flip to correct, how many flip the other way, and the added latency per generation.

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line
text: ← Day 36 · All 50 days · Day 38 →

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Write down the three power laws with their exponents and say what each one holds fixed.
Explain why a bare power law with no additive constant must be wrong at large N, and name the term Chinchilla adds tomorrow to fix it.
State how Kaplan counts N, and what the paper shows happens to the trend when embedding parameters are put back in.

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line
text:
Tasks 190 min


 50 min Read Section 1 - especially 1.2, the summary of scaling laws - and Section 2 for the notation and the derivation of C = 6ND. Copy the three exponents into your notes; you should find alpha_N around 0.076, alpha_D around 0.095 and alpha_C around 0.050, each with the range of N, D or C over which it was fitted.


 45 min Read Section 3 (the basic power laws, and why N excludes embedding parameters), move quickly through Sections 4 and 5, then read Section 6 properly: 6.1 gives the compute-optimal allocation N proportional to C^0.73 and D proportional to C^0.27, and 6.3 admits the trends must break somewhere.


 55 min Plot the fitted L(N) curve over the range Kaplan actually trained, then extrapolate it out to N = 1e15 and mark the point where the predicted loss crosses 1 nat per token. Write one sentence on what that crossing tells you about the functional form, given that text has nonzero entropy.


 40 min Write half a page as a referee: name the estimator (what is regressed on what, and in which space), list what is not reported (standard errors, residual structure, runs per point, sensitivity to the fitting range), and name the single claim you would ask the authors to weaken.

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line
text:
Resources

Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models paper

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line

A notebook with L(N) plotted over its fitted range and extrapolated well past it, the 1-nat crossing marked, and a half-page referee note on the fitting and the extrapolation.

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line
text:
Checkpoint

alpha_N is about 0.076. If you increase N tenfold and change nothing else, by what factor does the fitted L(N) say the loss falls, and is that a large effect or a small one?

title: Day 37 — Scaling laws, and how far you can trust a fitted line

title: Day 35 — Milestone 2: fine-tuned against prompted, on five axes

title: Day 35 — Milestone 2: fine-tuned against prompted, on five axes
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can say, for each of the five axes, which model wins and by how much, having scored the test split exactly once.
You can produce a reliability curve and an ECE for both models and say which one is overconfident, and in which probability band.
You have a failure taxonomy built by reading errors rather than by reading the confusion matrix, with counts, and at least one category that only one of the two models has.

title: Day 35 — Milestone 2: fine-tuned against prompted, on five axes
text:
Tasks 175 min


 35 min Score both models on the frozen test split, once. Report accuracy, macro-F1, per-class F1, and a confusion matrix for each. Then compare against the number you wrote down last night and note the direction of your miss.


 35 min Build reliability curves with 10 bins and compute ECE for both models. The fine-tune gives you softmax probabilities directly; for the prompted model use token logprobs if your provider returns them, and if it does not, use a verbalised confidence and state plainly in the report that it is a worse probability estimate, because it is.


 25 min Measure latency and cost: median and p95 seconds per abstract for each model, then cost per 1000 abstracts - token price times tokens for the prompted model, and for the fine-tune the GPU-hours you actually spent training, amortised over a stated volume, plus inference. State the volume you amortised over; the ranking changes completely between 1,000 and 1,000,000 abstracts.


 45 min Read 50 errors by hand, 25 from each model, inventing the categories as you go rather than deciding them in advance. Expect things like ‘secondary analysis of an RCT’, ‘protocol paper’, ‘design never named in the abstract’, ‘pooled cohort written up as a trial’. Count each category and mark which model it hits.


 35 min Write MILESTONE2.md. Spend the first ten minutes on the HELM Capabilities leaderboard and steal its layout: one row per metric, models as columns, and no aggregate score sitting at the top pretending to summarise them.

title: Day 33 — Project start: corpus, labels, split, metrics, baseline
text: ← Day 32 · All 50 days · Day 34 →

title: Day 33 — Project start: corpus, labels, split, metrics, baseline
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You have splits/{train,val,test}.pmids frozen with a recorded SHA-256 for each file, and you will not regenerate them before Day 50.
You can name your primary metric, say what it scores for a majority-class-always classifier on your actual label distribution, and defend why it and not raw accuracy is primary.
You have a prompting baseline number on a validation subsample from a model you did not train, with the exact prompt text, per-abstract latency, and token counts saved to disk.

title: Day 33 — Project start: corpus, labels, split, metrics, baseline
text:
Tasks 225 min


 60 min Pull the corpus with E-utilities: ESearch with usehistory=y to get WebEnv and query_key, then EFetch in batches of 200 PMIDs with retmode=xml, sleeping to stay under your rate limit. Target 8,000-10,000 abstracts. Parse PMID, title, abstract, journal, year, PublicationTypeList, and MeshHeadingList into data/abstracts.jsonl. Watch for structured abstracts - they carry several <AbstractText Label=...> elements, and taking only the first gives you the Background section instead of the paper.


 45 min Pick the task and write TASK.md. Default: five-way study design - RCT, cohort, case-control, review or meta-analysis, other. The alternative, if you have a live review protocol: binary first-pass screening, does this abstract report primary outcome data from a human study on your topic, yes or no. Take the five-way unless the screening task is something you will genuinely use this year, because its labels are partly recoverable from metadata and the screening task’s are not. Then write the silver-label rules: RCT and review/meta-analysis come from PublicationType, but cohort and case-control do not exist as publication types at all and must come from the MeSH headings Cohort Studies, Prospective Studies, and Case-Control Studies. Write an explicit precedence rule for abstracts that match two.


 35 min Freeze the split and choose metrics before any modelling: deduplicate near-identical abstracts first, then a stratified 70/15/15 split by silver label with a fixed seed, saved as three PMID lists with sha256sum recorded in TASK.md. Pick a primary metric - macro-F1 is usually the right answer once other dominates - and write down what it scores for a majority-class-always classifier.


 35 min Hand-label 100 abstracts from the test split yourself, reading only the abstract text, and record your disagreement rate against the silver label. That rate is a ceiling on anything you train later, so it goes in TASK.md rather than in a comment.


 50 min Get the prompting baseline: write baselines/prompt_v1.txt with your label definitions and one worked example per label, run it over 300 validation abstracts through an instruction-tuned model you have not fine-tuned, and save one row per abstract with prediction, raw output, wall-clock latency, and input/output token counts. Record your provider’s per-token price in baselines/prompt_v1_meta.json on the day you run it - Day 35 needs cost per 1000 abstracts, and prices move.

text:

If the gap is small, decide right now whether that is the model’s problem or the metric’s - you will not get an honest answer to this question once you have a fine-tuned model you like.

title: Day 31 — BERT, masked language modelling, and when an encoder still wins

title: Day 31 — BERT, masked language modelling, and when an encoder still wins
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can write down BERT’s masked-language-modelling loss and say why it does not give you a probability of a sentence the way your causal LM’s loss did.
You can state the conditions under which you would still put a 110M-parameter encoder into a biomedical classification pipeline in 2026 rather than prompting a large decoder, including at least one condition that is about latency or cost per 1000 abstracts rather than accuracy.
You can predict, then check, how a biomedical checkpoint and bert-base-uncased differ on The patient was treated with [MASK]., and explain the gap from what each one saw in pretraining.

title: Day 31 — BERT, masked language modelling, and when an encoder still wins

 55 min Read the BERT paper: abstract, section 1, section 3.1 (Pre-training BERT), section 3.2 (Fine-tuning BERT), Figure 1, and Table 1. Skim section 4’s result tables rather than studying them - the numbers are eight years stale, the recipe is not.

 35 min Write a 150-word note in your log titled ‘when I would still reach for an encoder’, ending in the decision rule you will apply to Day 33’s task. Commit to it - you are choosing the Day 34 model with it.

title: Day 27 — SLP3 Ch7 - the same model in prose
text: ← Day 26 · All 50 days · Day 28 →

title: Day 27 — SLP3 Ch7 - the same model in prose
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can name each decoding strategy in Ch7, say what it does to the probability distribution before a token is drawn, and identify from your Day 25 table which one produced the most readable Shakespeare.
You can convert your model’s val loss into perplexity and into bits per character, and explain why that number is not comparable to a BPE-level model’s perplexity.
You can point at the line of gpt_mine.py that implements the training objective Ch7 writes as an equation.

title: Day 27 — SLP3 Ch7 - the same model in prose
text:
Tasks 210 min


 60 min Read SLP3 Ch7 through the end of the decoding material with gpt_mine.py open, and locate each named thing in your code: the unembedding layer is your nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size), teacher forcing is why you never feed the model its own output during training, the context window is block_size, autoregressive conditioning is the loop in generate.


 45 min Implement top-p (nucleus) sampling in GPTLanguageModel.generate: sort logits descending, take the cumulative softmax, keep the smallest prefix whose mass exceeds p, mask the rest to -inf, renormalize. Run p over {0.9, 0.95, 1.0} on your best checkpoint and add the rows to the Day 25 decoding table.


 45 min Convert your val loss into the numbers Ch7’s evaluation section uses. Your loss is cross-entropy in nats per character, so a loss of 1.48 is exp(1.48) = 4.4 in perplexity and 1.48/ln(2) = 2.13 bits per character. Write in slp3_ch7_notes.md why a BPE-level model reporting perplexity 20 is not therefore worse, and what you would have to normalize by to compare them.


 30 min Read the pretraining-data section - what is actually in Common Crawl, what filtering and deduplication get applied before training. Write two sentences on what that implies for the PubMed abstract corpus you trained BPE on in Week 3 and will reuse from Day 33: what your filtering step would be, and what field you would deduplicate on.


 30 min Write the paragraph that joins the two halves of this week: which parts of Ch7 you had already implemented before you read them, and which parts the textbook has that your code does not - top-p until an hour ago, the KV cache, beam search, anything about the data.

title: Day 25 — Sweep the knobs, then sweep the decoder
text: ← Day 24 · All 50 days · Day 26 →

title: Day 25 — Sweep the knobs, then sweep the decoder
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can point at the step where val loss turns upward on your dropout=0.0 run while train loss keeps falling, and say why the dropout=0.2 run does not do that.
You can say what temperature 0.5 and top-k=1 do differently to a sample, given that both make it more conservative.
You can report which single hyperparameter change bought the most val loss per second of compute on your hardware, with the table row to back it.

title: Day 25 — Sweep the knobs, then sweep the decoder
text:
Tasks 215 min


 30 min Refactor gpt_mine.py so the configuration is a dict or argparse namespace and one function run(cfg) returns the train and val curves plus wall-clock seconds. Thirteen runs from the command line beats thirteen hand-edited copies of the file, and it stops you from misattributing a result to the wrong config.


 60 min Run a one-factor-at-a-time sweep off a fixed baseline (n_layer=4, n_head=4, n_embd=128, block_size=64, lr=1e-3, dropout=0.0, 1500 steps), varying block_size over {16, 64, 256}, n_layer over {1, 4, 8}, n_head over {1, 4, 8}, n_embd over {64, 128, 384}, lr over {1e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3}, dropout over {0.0, 0.2, 0.5}. Log train and val loss every 100 steps to CSV. The full grid is 729 runs; you are doing 13 and you should be able to say what that costs you.


 40 min Plot the curves, one panel per swept factor, train and val on the same axes with distinguishable line styles. Label the y-axis ‘cross-entropy loss (nats per character)’, not ‘loss’. Mark the step where each val curve turns up.


 40 min Sweep decoding on your single best checkpoint: temperature over {0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.3} with top-k off, then top-k over {1, 10, 50, None} at temperature 1.0. Same seed, same 20-character prompt, 200 tokens each.


 45 min Write experiments.md with one table for the sweep (config, parameter count, final train loss, final val loss, seconds) and one for decoding (setting, a 20-token excerpt, one sentence of judgement). End with the single sentence: the change that bought the most val loss per second was X, worth Y nats for Z seconds.

What you should be able to do tonight

title: Day 19 — Attention from the textbook: Q, K, V and the sqrt(d_k)
text: ← Day 18 · All 50 days · Day 20 →

title: Day 19 — Attention from the textbook: Q, K, V and the sqrt(d_k)
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can explain the sqrt(d_k) divisor from the variance of a dot product, and say what breaks at d_k = 64 without it.
You can write causal masking in three lines and say why the mask is applied to the scores before the softmax rather than to the weights after it.
You can count the parameters in one transformer block at d_model = 384 with 6 heads, and say what fraction sits in the feed-forward sublayer rather than in attention.

title: Day 19 — Attention from the textbook: Q, K, V and the sqrt(d_k)
text:
Tasks 200 min


 70 min Read SLP3 Ch8 through single-head attention. When you reach the scaling factor, stop and do the algebra yourself: for two independent d_k-vectors with unit-variance entries, the dot product has variance d_k, so dividing by sqrt(d_k) puts the scores back at unit variance. Then write what a variance of 64 does to a softmax.


 50 min Read multi-head attention and causal masking. Work out on paper why the mask is a lower-triangular fill with negative infinity and not a multiply by zero, and what each row of the attention matrix has to sum to.


 45 min Read the input and output ends: token embeddings plus positional embeddings, the residual stream, where layer norm sits, the feed-forward sublayer with its 4x expansion, the unembedding matrix and weight tying.


 35 min Write one attention head as five matrix operations with a shape on every intermediate, for batch 32, context 256, d_model 384, 6 heads. Then write out the causal mask explicitly for T = 4 and sanity-check that row 2 attends to exactly three positions.

title: Day 9 — Perplexity, and what smoothing is actually for
text:

title: Day 5 — Softmax regression is multinomial logistic regression
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Show that minimizing cross-entropy against one-hot labels is exactly maximizing the multinomial log-likelihood, in three lines of algebra.
Explain why exp(o) returns inf above roughly 88.7 in float32 and roughly 709.8 in float64, and prove that subtracting max(o) leaves softmax(o) unchanged.
Derive dL/do = yhat - y for softmax plus cross-entropy and verify it numerically against autograd.

title: Day 5 — Softmax regression is multinomial logistic regression
text:
Tasks 220 min


 50 min Read D2L 4.1 Softmax Regression in full, including the Log-Likelihood subsection, the Softmax and Cross-Entropy Loss subsection, and the information theory section. Where D2L writes cross-entropy, write NLL in the margin - they are the same object and you should stop translating between them by the end of today.


 40 min Write softmax(X) twice: the naive version exp(X) / exp(X).sum(1, keepdim=True), and the stable version that subtracts X.max(1, keepdim=True).values first. Feed both a float32 logit row containing 100.0 and record exactly what each returns.


 40 min Implement cross-entropy directly from logits as logsumexp(o) - o[y], never forming probabilities first, and check it agrees with torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy to 1e-6 on a random batch of shape (64, 10).


 60 min Train softmax regression on FashionMNIST from scratch - your softmax, your loss, manual SGD on W of shape (784, 10) and b of shape (10,) - for 10 epochs, and print test accuracy. Low-to-mid 80s is the expected number. If you are sitting at 10% you have a sign error, not a learning-rate problem.


 30 min Derive dL/do = softmax(o) - onehot(y) on paper for a single example, then check it: build a random logit vector with requires_grad=True, compute your loss, call backward(), and compare o.grad against softmax(o) - onehot(y).

text:

 30 min Reproduce the numerical-derivative section yourself: define f(x) = 3x^2 - 4x + 5, compute (f(x+h) - f(x)) / h at x = 3 for h in [1e-1, 1e-3, 1e-6, 1e-12], and plot the absolute error against the analytic value 6x - 4 = 14. Explain in one sentence why h = 1e-12 is worse than h = 1e-6.

Fill in every .grad on paper, then check against the video. o comes out at 0.7071, so do/dn = 1 - o^2 = 0.5 exactly - that is the number every other gradient is scaled by.

title: Day 1 — Tensors, gradients, and the chain rule
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Derive the gradient of ||y - Xb||^2 with respect to b and land on -2X’(y - Xb) using only the identities from D2L 2.4, not memory.
Predict the output shape of A.sum(axis=0), A.sum(axis=1), and A.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) for A of shape (5, 4) before running any of them.

title: Day 1 — Tensors, gradients, and the chain rule
text:
Tasks 205 min


 65 min Read D2L 2.3 Linear Algebra in a notebook, typing every code cell instead of reading past it. Stop at every axis= argument and say the output shape out loud before you run the cell, especially where keepdims=True appears.

 40 min Derive d/db of ||y - Xb||^2 on paper using only those identities, and confirm you get -2X’(y - Xb). You already know the closed-form OLS solution; the point is that today it falls out of the chain rule rather than out of a textbook box.


 45 min Implement numerical_gradient(f, x, eps=1e-5) using the central difference (f(x+eps) - f(x-eps)) / (2*eps) and check it against your analytic OLS gradient on a random X of shape (100, 5). Print the max absolute difference: it should sit at 1e-7 or below. Then rerun with eps=1e-12 and watch it get worse.

title: Self-check
text:
Use the phase blocks as you go — each one belongs on that phase’s catch-up day. The final list is day 50.
The rule is the same throughout: say the answer out loud before you look. Recognising a correct explanation is easy and feels identical to knowing it, which is exactly why it’s a bad test.

title: Self-check
text:
After week 1 — Foundations

Write the chain rule for a scalar composed of three functions, then say what reverse-mode differentiation computes that forward-mode doesn’t, and why that ordering matters when there are a million parameters and one loss.
What does loss.backward() actually do to the graph, and why do you have to zero gradients between steps? What goes wrong if you forget?
Softmax regression is a model you already know under a different name. Which one, and what is cross-entropy in that language?
Why does a naive exp(logits) overflow, and what does subtracting the max fix?
Derive the gradient of cross-entropy with respect to the logits. It collapses to something very short — what, and why is that convenient?
Why does initialization matter at all if the network is going to learn the weights anyway?

title: Self-check
text:
After week 2 — Language models

What exactly is a language model estimating? Write the probability of a sentence under a bigram model.
Perplexity — define it, and state its relationship to cross-entropy loss in one line.
Why is a maximum-likelihood n-gram model useless on held-out text without smoothing? What does smoothing actually trade away?
A bigram count table and a one-layer neural network trained with gradient descent give the same answer. Why? What does the neural version buy you that the table doesn’t?
What does the embedding table in an MLP language model represent, and why is it better than one-hot?
Your loss plateaus and the activation histograms are saturated at ±1. What happened, and what are two fixes?

title: Self-check
text:
After week 3 — Tokens and attention

What is a token? Why is “how many r’s in strawberry” hard, and why do numbers behave oddly?
Walk through BPE training: what does one merge step do, and what determines the final vocabulary?
Attention uses queries, keys and values. Say what each one is for, in one sentence each, without using the words “attend” or “attention”.
Why is the dot product divided by the square root of the head dimension? What breaks without it?
What does causal masking do, and at what point in the computation is it applied?
Why do transformers need positional information when RNNs don’t?
Name three things the 2017 paper does that a modern decoder-only model doesn’t.

title: Self-check
text:
After week 4 — Build a GPT

Trace one forward pass from token ids to logits, naming every tensor shape, for a stated batch size, context length, d_model and head count.
Where does the residual stream go, and why does removing the residual connections break training?
What is LayerNorm normalizing over, and why pre-norm rather than post-norm?
Derive your model’s parameter count from its hyperparameters. Which component dominates, and when?
What do temperature and top-k each do to the output distribution? At temperature 0, what is the model?
Your training loss falls and validation loss rises. Name three causes and how you’d tell them apart.

title: Self-check
text:
After week 5 — Pretrained models

Encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder — what training objective goes with each, and what task shape does each suit?
For classifying 5,000 biomedical abstracts, when is a BERT-family encoder still the right answer?
What is an attention mask for, and what breaks silently if you pad without one?
Why is a prompting baseline worth a whole day before any fine-tuning?
Your fine-tuned model scores better than the prompted one on accuracy but you don’t want to ship it. Give two reasons that can be true.

title: Self-check
text:
After week 6 — Scaling and post-training

State what scaling laws claim, and one thing they explicitly don’t predict.
Where do Kaplan and Chinchilla disagree, and what changed in practice as a result?
For a fixed compute budget, how do you split it between parameters and tokens? Show the arithmetic.
Why is a pretrained base model not an assistant? Name the three InstructGPT stages and what each fixes.
What is the alignment tax?
LoRA freezes the base weights and learns a low-rank update. What do rank r and alpha control, and why does this save so much memory when the parameter saving alone doesn’t explain it?

title: Self-check
text:
After week 7 — Pipeline, RAG, evaluation

DPO removes the reward model and the RL loop. What is the key reparameterization that lets it?
Order the stages of building a chat model from raw text, and say what each contributes.
When does retrieval beat fine-tuning, and when is reaching for retrieval hiding a different problem?
Why must retrieval quality be measured separately from end-to-end answer quality?
Give a case where a model scores well on a benchmark and is still unfit for clinical use.
Distinguish model failure from retrieval failure from interface failure, with an example of each.

title: Self-check
text:
Day 50 — the whole thing
The list the bootcamp was designed around. Eight questions, no notes.

What is a token?
Why do models predict the next token?
What do embeddings represent?
Why does attention use queries, keys and values?
What does causal masking do?
Why do transformers need positional information?
How does pretraining differ from post-training?
Why is perplexity useful but insufficient?

And three you should be able to do, not just say:

Implement a decoder-only transformer from an empty file. You did this on day 24 — do it again.
Fine-tune a pretrained model on a task you define, and report more than accuracy.
Design an evaluation for a clinical task that would catch a polished, confident, wrong answer.

Anything on this page you can’t answer is not a failure. It’s your October reading list — and CS336 is where most of the remaining answers live.

 45 min Run the control-flow example from 2.5: f(a) with a while b.norm() < 1000 loop and an if b.sum() > 0 branch. Call backward() and confirm a.grad == d / a. Then write two sentences explaining why that identity holds - for any fixed a, f is linear in a with some constant k, so d = k*a.


 30 min Build the accumulation bug on purpose: take a scalar parameter, run backward() twice without zero_(), and print .grad after each call. Write down the number you would have expected if gradients overwrote instead of accumulated. Keep this cell - you will hit this for real on Day 4.


 25 min Call backward() twice on the same graph, read the RuntimeError about backward through the graph a second time, then fix it with retain_graph=True and write one line on what that costs you in memory.

title: Day 8 — A language model you can count by hand
text:
Tasks 205 min


 65 min Read SLP3 Ch3 sections 3.1-3.4. Before you reach the MLE equation, stop and maximize the multinomial log-likelihood yourself on paper - it is the same estimator you use for allele frequencies, with the same Lagrange-multiplier normalization step.


 55 min Clone github.com/karpathy/makemore, confirm names.txt loads and count the entries (32,033), then build a 27x27 integer tensor N of character bigram counts using ‘.’ as index 0 for both start and end of a name.

title: Day 28 — Milestone 1 - My GPT from scratch
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

title: Day 28 — Milestone 1 - My GPT from scratch
text:
Tasks 190 min


 40 min Derive the parameter count by hand before running anything: token embedding VE, position embedding TE, then per block 4E^2 for attention (three projections plus the output projection) and 8E^2 for the feed-forward, plus biases and two LayerNorms, then the final LayerNorm and the LM head. Notice what the 4 to 8 ratio means - the feed-forward holds twice the parameters attention does, in every block.


 25 min Check the derivation against the number you wrote down last night. For n_layer=6, n_head=6, n_embd=384, block_size=256 on a 65-character vocabulary the video’s script prints about 10.79 M, and your algebra should land on the same figure to the digit. Any mismatch names the term you forgot: a bias, the second LayerNorm, the position table. Do not move on until they agree.


 60 min Assemble milestone-1.md: the architecture drawn with tensor shapes on every arrow from (B,T) to (B,T,vocab_size), the parameter table broken down by component with each component’s share as a percentage, the Day 25 sweep table, the decoding table including top-p, three annotated samples, and your val loss beside nanoGPT’s ~1.47 on the same data.


 35 min Write the ‘what surprised me’ section with at least four specific items, each with its evidence attached. Candidates from this week, to be replaced with your own: the feed-forward outweighs attention two to one in parameters; removing residuals cost more than removing LayerNorm; the whole model is roughly 200 lines; top-k=1 collapses into a repeating loop inside 100 tokens.


 30 min Reread the whole thing as a hostile reviewer. Every claim needs a number or a file line behind it, every plot axis needs units, every rounded figure needs consistent precision. Anything you believe but did not test goes into a closing ‘not verified’ section. Then send it to someone.

 45 min Write the reconciliation with yesterday: the two allocation exponent sets side by side (Kaplan N ~ C^0.73 and D ~ C^0.27; Chinchilla roughly N ~ C^0.5 and D ~ C^0.5), then the reasons Chinchilla gives - the learning-rate schedule length held fixed regardless of token count, and the exclusion of embedding parameters from N. Add one line on whether the parametric fit in 3.3 carries the uncertainty quantification you asked for yesterday; a later replication attempt could not reproduce that fit from the paper’s own numbers.

title: Day 46 — Retrieval: BM25, bi-encoders, and when RAG is the wrong answer
text: ← Day 45 · All 50 days · Day 47 →

title: Day 46 — Retrieval: BM25, bi-encoders, and when RAG is the wrong answer
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can write the BM25 score for a single query term by hand and say what k1 and b each control.
You can state the difference between a bi-encoder and a cross-encoder, and why only one of them can be precomputed into a FAISS index.
You can name two conditions under which fine-tuning beats retrieval on your abstract task, and two under which retrieval wins.

title: Day 46 — Retrieval: BM25, bi-encoders, and when RAG is the wrong answer
text:
Tasks 200 min


 55 min Read SLP3 Ch11 through the classical IR material: tf-idf, the BM25 scoring function, inverted indexes, and the standard IR metrics. Pay attention to how BM25 saturates term frequency - it is a fixed nonlinearity someone chose, not something estimated, and that is worth noticing.


 45 min Read the rest of Ch11: dense retrieval with bi-encoders, approximate nearest-neighbour search, chunking, and the RAG section. Write down every design choice the chapter treats as free that you think is actually load-bearing for a corpus of 250-word abstracts.


 45 min Read the RAG paper (arXiv 2005.11401). Separate RAG-Sequence from RAG-Token, and be precise about which components were trained and which were frozen - the document encoder and the index stay fixed while the query encoder and generator are fine-tuned, and that asymmetry is the whole engineering story.


 30 min Implement BM25 in about 20 lines of numpy over 200 of your PubMed abstracts. Query it with five real questions, then sweep k1 over {0.5, 1.2, 2.0} and b over {0, 0.75, 1.0} and record how the top-5 changes.


 25 min Write a one-page decision note for your abstract task: what fine-tuning can do that retrieval cannot, what retrieval can do that fine-tuning cannot, and two concrete failure modes where adding retrieval makes a knowledge problem look solved without solving it.

title: Day 48 — HELM, MedHELM, and the distinctions a single score erases
text: ← Day 47 · All 50 days · Day 49 →

title: Day 48 — HELM, MedHELM, and the distinctions a single score erases
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

You can name the metric categories HELM measures for every scenario and say which of them your Day 35 report already covers.
You can point at two MedHELM scenarios and say precisely what a high score there does and does not predict about a clinician’s actual workflow.
You can classify a given failure as a model failure, a retrieval failure, an interface failure or a workflow failure, and name the evidence that settles it.

title: Day 48 — HELM, MedHELM, and the distinctions a single score erases
text:
Tasks 205 min


 55 min Read the HELM paper (arXiv 2211.09110) Sections 1-3: the scenario-by-metric taxonomy, the argument for dense coverage over cherry-picked benchmarks, and the seven metric categories measured for each scenario - accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency. Write down which of the seven you have ever measured on your own task.


 35 min Open HELM Capabilities and HELM Lite. Pick one model and read across its metrics instead of down the ranking, then find a pair of models whose order flips depending on which metric you sort by. Record the pair and the two metrics.


 50 min Work through MedHELM. Count how many of its scenarios are built from real clinical records or clinician-authored workflows versus how many are exam-style multiple choice, then pick the two scenarios closest to your PubMed abstract task and write down what each actually asks the model to do.


 45 min Write eval_principles.md: six distinctions - capability vs reliability, average vs worst case, knowledge vs reasoning, correctness vs persuasiveness, benchmark score vs real-world utility, and model failure vs retrieval/interface/workflow failure. For each, one line on which side your current evaluation measures, and one line on what you would have to build to measure the other.


 20 min Record what maintenance mode since 2026-06-01 means for using HELM going forward: which parts stay useful indefinitely (the taxonomy, the scenario definitions, the released code) and which decay (leaderboard coverage of anything released after that date).

title: Day 50 — Capstone - the final report, the closed-book self-assessment, and what comes next

title: Day 50 — Capstone - the final report, the closed-book self-assessment, and what comes next
section: What you should be able to do tonight
text:
What you should be able to do tonight

Your report states one recommendation for the abstract task and names the specific result that would reverse it.
You answered all eight self-assessment questions closed-book, then checked each against the source and marked the ones that were thin.
Your roadmap names a dated next commitment - CS336 assignment 1 with a start date - rather than an intention to keep learning.

title: Day 50 — Capstone - the final report, the closed-book self-assessment, and what comes next
text:
Tasks 195 min


 70 min Write final_report.md from the Day 49 harness output: one table with every approach - prompted, fine-tuned, LoRA, RAG - crossed with every metric, the error taxonomy with counts, and a recommendation stated as a decision plus the condition that would reverse it. Carry the RAG results as two tables, retrieval and end-to-end, exactly as on Day 47.


 60 min Sit the self-assessment closed-book. Write roughly five minutes on each: what a token is; why models are trained to predict the next token; what an embedding represents; why attention uses queries, keys and values rather than one vector per position; what causal masking does and what breaks without it; why a transformer needs positional information at all; how pretraining differs from post-training; and why perplexity is useful but insufficient. Then check every answer against the source and mark the thin ones - the marks are the point.


 30 min Write the limitations section of the report and tie each limitation to a specific analysis rather than to research in general: which split it came from, how many examples, what the metric cannot distinguish, and which claim you would withdraw if one number moved.


 35 min Write roadmap.md with three parts. CS336: open the site, the lecture playlist and assignment1-basics, and put a start date on assignment 1 - it rebuilds the tokenizer, transformer and optimizer from scratch, which is where your thin marks get fixed. Agents: write down what an agent that ran a literature search and drafted an MR analysis plan would need that you cannot yet build. Medical LLM literature: run one PubMed search on clinical LLM evaluation, save ten papers, and read the abstracts.

title: Milestones
text: Days pass and leave nothing behind unless you write something down. These five are the artifacts the bootcamp is actually for — the rest of the days feed them.

title: Milestones
section: Milestone 1 — A GPT you wrote yourself
text:
Milestone 1 — A GPT you wrote yourself
Day 28, Thursday 20 August. Fed by days 22–27.
A decoder-only transformer you implemented, trained, and understand line by line, written up as a short report.
What’s in it

The architecture, with your parameter count derived by hand and then checked against the code. If the two numbers disagree, the report is finding out why.
The experiment table from day 25: context length, n_layer, n_head, n_embd, learning rate and dropout against final validation loss. Say which change mattered and which didn’t.
Samples at three sampling temperatures, and one sentence on what temperature is actually doing to the distribution.
A short section on what nanoGPT does that yours doesn’t, and why those things exist.

How you know it’s good — you can hand someone your model.py, and answer any “why is this line here?” question about it without looking anything up.

title: Milestones
section: Milestone 2 — Fine-tuned versus prompted, evaluated properly
text:
Milestone 2 — Fine-tuned versus prompted, evaluated properly
Day 35, Thursday 27 August. Fed by days 29–34.
The first real report on the PubMed abstract task. The point of this one is that it is not a single accuracy number, because a single accuracy number is how clinical AI results get overclaimed.
What’s in it

The task, the label scheme, and the train/validation/test split — fixed here and not touched again for the rest of the bootcamp.
Accuracy and per-class performance for the prompting baseline and the fine-tune. Confidence intervals, since you know how to compute them.
Calibration: a reliability curve and expected calibration error. A model that is 95% confident and 70% correct is a different failure from one that is 70% confident and 70% correct.
Latency and cost per 1000 abstracts for both approaches.
A failure taxonomy you built by reading actual errors — not a confusion matrix, a set of named failure modes with an example of each.

How you know it’s good — it contains at least one finding that would make you not deploy the better-scoring model.

title: Milestones
text:
Milestone 3 — The whole pipeline, traced
Day 45, Sunday 6 September. Fed by day 44.
nanochat is the only place you’ll see every stage in one readable repo: tokenizer training, pretraining, mid-training, SFT, RL, evaluation, and serving. The deliverable is a written trace of the path from raw text to a model answering in a browser.
What’s in it

Each stage, the script that runs it, what goes in, what comes out, and roughly what it costs in GPU-hours.
For each stage, the thing you built earlier that corresponds to it — your BPE from day 18, your training loop from day 24, your fine-tune from day 34.
The two or three stages you could not have guessed were necessary before reading it.

Running the speedrun yourself is optional. Reading it properly is not.

title: Milestones
section: Milestone 4 — Retrieval over your own corpus
text:
Milestone 4 — Retrieval over your own corpus
Day 47, Tuesday 8 September. Fed by day 46.
A working RAG system over the PubMed abstracts you’ve been carrying since day 18.
What’s in it

Chunking strategy and why you chose it. Abstracts are short — say whether chunking helped at all.
Embeddings, an index, and top-k retrieval.
Retrieval quality measured separately: recall@k and MRR against a small set of questions where you know which abstract holds the answer.
End-to-end answer quality measured separately.
One worked example where retrieval succeeded and generation still got it wrong, and one of the reverse.

How you know it’s good — you can say which of your end-to-end failures are retrieval failures and which are generation failures. Conflating the two is the single most common way RAG evaluations mislead.

title: Milestones
text:
Milestone 5 — Capstone
Day 50, Friday 11 September.
Two things.
The final report pulls together days 33 to 49 into one evaluation of the biomedical task across every approach you tried — zero-shot prompt, few-shot prompt, full fine-tune, LoRA, and RAG — on the same fixed test set, with the same metrics, plus the error taxonomy. It ends with a recommendation and the conditions under which you’d change it.
The self-assessment is the self-check page, answered out loud, without notes. Anything you can’t answer is not a failure of the bootcamp; it’s the reading list for October.

title: Setup
section: Still yours to do — about five minutes
text:
Still yours to do — about five minutes
Two accounts, because they need a browser and a password you own:

Hugging Face — needed from day 29. Create an access token under Settings → Access Tokens, then hf auth login. (huggingface-cli login still works but is deprecated in huggingface_hub 1.24, which is what’s installed.)
Weights & Biases — free for personal use, and it’s what makes day 25’s experiment table and the milestone write-ups easy. Then wandb login.

Optional: an API key for a hosted model (Anthropic or OpenAI) makes day 33’s prompting baseline quicker. An open instruction-tuned model from the Hub works too.

title: Setup
section: The CUDA trap, and why the install is pinned
text:
The CUDA trap, and why the install is pinned
Your driver is 573.57, advertising CUDA 12.8. The GPU is a GTX 1650 Ti (Turing, sm_75).
Installing torch from PyPI the obvious way gets you a CUDA 13 build, which does not run on a 12.8 driver. It does not fail loudly either — it installs cleanly and then reports:
torch 2.13.0+cu130 | cuda available: False
which is the kind of thing you discover on day 25 when a sweep takes all afternoon on CPU. So the environment pins the CUDA 12.8 build explicitly:
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu128 torch
If you ever rebuild the environment, use that line rather than a bare uv pip install torch.






Sanity check before any GPU day: python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())" must print True. If it prints False, you are about to train on CPU.

title: Setup
text:
Rebuilding the environment from scratch
uv venv ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp --python 3.12
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu128 torch
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  -r /mnt/c/Users/csu/Desktop/projects/llm-bootcamp-work/requirements.txt
The environment deliberately lives at ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp, on the Linux filesystem, rather than beside the code under /mnt/c. WSL2’s bridge to the Windows drive is slow for operations over thousands of small files, which is exactly what a PyTorch venv is. Code and notebooks on /mnt/c are fine; the venv is not.

title: Setup
section: What your 4 GB of VRAM will and won’t do
text:
What your 4 GB of VRAM will and won’t do
This decides which days run on your laptop and which want Colab.








Weeks
Locally?
Notes




1–4
Comfortably
micrograd and makemore barely touch the GPU. The day 22–26 GPT is about 10.8 M parameters at the video’s config and trains on TinyShakespeare in minutes. Day 25’s sweeps are the first thing that really benefits from the card.


5
Tight but workable
A BERT-family encoder fine-tune fits with a modest batch size and mixed precision. Reach for gradient accumulation before reaching for Colab.


6
Depends
Day 41’s LoRA run is where 4 GB starts choosing your model for you. Small model plus LoRA plus 4-bit fits; a full fine-tune of anything decoder-shaped probably doesn’t.


7
Embeddings yes, speedrun no
Day 47’s retrieval work runs locally. Day 45’s nanochat speedrun needs 8×H100 — it is explicitly optional, and reading the pipeline carefully is the point.



When something doesn’t fit, Colab’s free T4 has 16 GB and covers everything in weeks 5–7.

title: Setup
text:
Verify
If this prints a decreasing loss and cuda: True, you are ready for day 1:
import torch
print("cuda:", torch.cuda.is_available())

x = torch.randn(64, 3)
y = (x.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True) > 0).float()
w = torch.zeros(3, 1, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.zeros(1, requires_grad=True)

for step in range(200):
    loss = torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(x @ w + b, y)
    loss.backward()
    with torch.no_grad():
        w -= 0.5 * w.grad; b -= 0.5 * b.grad
        w.grad = None; b.grad = None
    if step % 50 == 0:
        print(step, round(loss.item(), 4))
You will not fully understand loss.backward() yet. That is days 2 through 4.
